title: Margherita Pizza (Tawa and Oven Method)

subs.name: atta (whole wheat flour) for a heartier base
subs.notes: the dough will be slightly denser; add a little extra water as atta absorbs more

subs.name: 1 tsp baking powder mixed into the flour for a quick dough — no rest time needed
subs.notes: the base will be more bready than chewy but perfectly edible

ingredients.name: ripe tomatoes, blended or canned tomatoes, crushed
fresh mozzarella or pizza cheese (block), torn or sliced

subs.name: crumbled paneer + 2 tbsp cream
subs.notes: pan-fry paneer briefly first so it holds its shape under heat

subs.name: fresh coriander or mint leaves scattered at the end
subs.notes: scatter only after the pizza comes off heat — they wilt and taste better

steps.text: Mix yeast, sugar and 50 ml of the warm water in a small bowl. Leave for 5–10 minutes until it turns frothy — if it does not froth, the yeast is dead and must be replaced.

Combine flour and salt in a wide bowl, make a well in the centre and pour in the yeast mixture, remaining water and olive oil. Mix until a shaggy dough forms, then knead for 8–10 minutes until smooth and elastic. The dough should spring back when you poke it.
Shape into a ball, place in a lightly oiled bowl, cover with a damp cloth and rest in a warm spot for 60 minutes until the dough has doubled in size.
Meanwhile, prepare the sauce: cook the blended tomatoes with a pinch of salt and the garlic (if using) in a small pan for 8–10 minutes until slightly thickened. Remove from heat and cool.

Punch down the risen dough and divide into 4 equal portions. On a lightly floured surface, stretch each portion by hand or with a rolling pin into a thin round about 25 cm across. Do not use too much flour or the base will not crisp.

OVEN METHOD — Preheat oven to its highest setting (240–250 °C) for 30 minutes with a baking tray inside. Slide one pizza onto the hot tray, spread 3 tbsp sauce to within 1 cm of the edge, top with cheese pieces and bake for 8–10 minutes until the base is blistered and the cheese is molten and beginning to brown.

TAWA METHOD — Heat a dry iron tawa on high flame until very hot. Place the stretched base on the tawa. After 1 minute the underside will set and small bubbles will appear. Spread sauce and cheese, cover the tawa with a lid and cook on medium-low for 5–7 minutes until the cheese melts and the base is cooked through. For a slightly charred top, hold the lid slightly ajar and place a burning charcoal or tongs-held flame beneath the lid for 30 seconds.

Remove from the oven or tawa, scatter fresh basil leaves immediately over the hot cheese, drizzle with a thread of olive oil and serve without cutting into too many slices — Neapolitan pizza is meant to be eaten soft and floppy.

cross_contact_notes: Ingredient metadata cannot prove an allergen-free kitchen. Check packaged labels and cross-contact statements for your products.
safety_warnings: Fermentation conditions affect safety. Discard batches with unexpected mould, putrid odour or other spoilage signs.
trust.provenance_summary: AI-assisted original draft; declared licence original.
trust.substitution_warning: A substitution can introduce allergens or change dietary suitability. Never claim a swap is safe without checking the replacement's label and the user's allergy context.

substitution_notes: yeast → 1 tsp baking powder mixed into the flour for a quick dough — no rest time needed (the base will be more bready than chewy but perfectly edible); maida → atta (whole wheat flour) for a heartier base (the dough will be slightly denser; add a little extra water as atta absorbs more); cheese → crumbled paneer + 2 tbsp cream (pan-fry paneer briefly first so it holds its shape under heat); basil → fresh coriander or mint leaves scattered at the end (scatter only after the pizza comes off heat — they wilt and taste better)
indian_kitchen_adaptation: The tawa method is the easiest route without an oven — a heavy iron tawa (available at any housewares store) replicates the crisp base. Amul pizza cheese or any block mozzarella (sold at Nature's Basket and online) grates and melts well. Maida is available at every kirana store. Fresh basil is increasingly available at metro supermarkets; dried basil can be sprinkled before topping instead.
